nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with rocky substrates; also found in floodplain lakes and flooded forest margins during the wet season. Prefers well-oxygenated, clear to slightly turbid water.

decorPreferences: large smooth river rocks and boulders
driftwood and root structures for cover
minimal or no live plants (plants will be eaten)
robust artificial plants if planting is desired
powerful filtration and strong water flow

diet.notes: Heavily plant-based in the wild — feeds primarily on plant matter, algae, soft-leaved aquatic vegetation, fallen fruits, and filamentous algae. In captivity, accepts sinking algae wafers, spirulina-based foods, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, lettuce, spinach), and vegetable flake. Will also take high-quality sinking pellets, occasional frozen foods (bloodworm, brine shrimp), and live invertebrates. Any soft-leaved aquarium plants will be consumed. Feed two to three times daily with predominantly plant-based foods.

goodWith: large robust cichlids (oscars, severums, large eartheaters)
other large robust characins of similar size
avoidWith: long-finned fish of any species (aggressive fin-nipper)
angelfish and discus (fins will be nipped severely)
tetras, rasboras, and other small schooling fish
shy or retiring species that cannot defend themselves

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image of Leporinus fasciatus showing characteristic black-and-yellow banding pattern on an adult specimen.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult banding pattern. Good alternative if FishBase image is not available for reuse.

summary: A striking large South American characin instantly recognized by its bold yellow-and-black horizontal banding. Juveniles display around 5 bands; adults develop 10 or more. Notorious fin-nipper and vigorous plant-eater that swims head-down at a distinctive 45° angle — a trait shared across family Anostomidae. Needs 400 L+ and robust tankmates.
careNotes:
The Banded Leporinus is one of the most visually impressive large South American characins, but it is frequently mistreated in the hobby because juveniles (sold at 5–8 cm with 5 visible bands) look harmless and are inexpensive. Within 12–18 months they can reach 20–25 cm and require tank upgrades most community aquarists are unwilling to make. A minimum 400 L tank with at least 150 cm footprint is strongly recommended for a single adult or a small group of three or more; groups tend to distribute aggression more evenly than pairs.

The characteristic head-down, 45° swimming posture is normal for all Anostomidae and is not a sign of illness. This posture allows the fish to browse algae and plant matter from vertical rock surfaces in their native river habitat. In the aquarium the fish will similarly rest and feed at odd angles, particularly near decor.

Banding increases with age: juveniles typically display around 5 bold black-and-yellow bands; as the fish grows, intermediate bands develop between the originals until adults carry 10 or more bands. This change is gradual and spans several years.

This species is an aggressive and indiscriminate fin-nipper. It will shred the fins of angelfish, discus, bettas, and any long-finned variety regardless of relative size. Even tankmates significantly larger than the Leporinus are not always safe. Only very robust, short-finned tankmates should be considered. Similarly, any soft-leaved plant in the aquarium will be consumed; decor should consist of rocks, driftwood, and robust artificial plants only.

Water quality must be high with good oxygenation and moderate-to-strong circulation to replicate the fast-flowing river conditions of the wild habitat. Softer, slightly acidic water is preferred, though the species tolerates a moderate pH range. The Banded Leporinus is a strong, confident jumper — a tightly fitting, weighted lid is essential, as escapes are common especially at night or when the fish is startled.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. In the wild, Leporinus fasciatus undertakes seasonal spawning migrations to flooded forest areas during high-water periods. Captive breeding requires large, species-specific setups with simulated seasonal changes (temperature drop, increased water flow, and water level fluctuation). Eggs are scattered among substrate and receive no parental care. Commercial specimens are wild-caught or farm-raised in large outdoor ponds in tropical countries. Breeding difficulty is considered advanced to not in captivity for the home aquarist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Leporinus fasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 30 cm TL), temperature range 22–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 2–15 dH, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins and beyond), habitat (fast-flowing rocky rivers), diet (plant matter and algae), behavioural notes (fin-nipper, head-down swimming posture), and minimum tank recommendation.

additional.name: FishBase — Leporinus fasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (max 30 cm TL), taxonomy (Anostomidae, Characiformes), native range (South America broadly including Trinidad), diet classification (herbivore/omnivore with plant matter dominant), and lifespan data. Confirmed banding ontogeny (band count increases with age) from species notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Leporinus Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide. Confirmed fin-nipper reputation, plant-eating behavior, minimum tank size guidance (100+ gallons / ~400 L), head-down swimming posture as Anostomidae trait, and jumpiness requiring covered tanks. Noted that juveniles are commonly sold in community fish sections despite being unsuitable for most community setups.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Leporinus fasciatus species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming fin-nipping aggression, diet composition, minimum tank requirements, and that band count increases from 5 in juveniles to 10+ in adults. Also noted the species’ preference for robust tankmates and risk of outgrowing community aquaria.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Leporinus fasciatus
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist reference. Confirmed South American river biotope preference, noted the head-down posture as diagnostic of Anostomidae, and highlighted the mismatch between juvenile appearance at point of sale and adult size/behavior requirements.

fish.breedingNotes: Seasonal spawning migrant in the wild; captive breeding is extremely rare and requires very large setups with simulated seasonal cues. Essentially not achievable in home aquaria — commercial stock is wild-caught or pond-raised.
